programs.name: Senior Advocate Program
programs.what_it_does: The program employs a dedicated staff member at the McMinn Senior Activity Center to assist older adults in navigating bureaucratic red tape for services like food, home repairs, and other aid they might not otherwise qualify for. It operates at a grassroots level, partnering with local entities such as housing authorities, VA, AARP, SHIP, and Social Security offices.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults age 50 and up in McMinn County, Tennessee, particularly those facing barriers to standard assistance programs.[1]
programs.whats_unique: One of only three such programs in Tennessee with a dedicated staff member, and the only one in East Tennessee, emphasizing local, hands-on advocacy beyond typical state or federal services.[1]
programs.benefit_value: Access to otherwise unavailable resources through personalized advocacy; serves 562 senior center members in a county with 15,033 residents aged 60+.[1]

programs.name: OPTIONS Program
programs.what_it_does: This state-funded program provides Homemaker services, Home Delivered Meals, and Personal Care Assistance to support seniors in remaining at home. It is accessed through Area Agencies on Aging and Disability as part of Tennessee's long-term care framework.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Older adults and those with disabilities in Southwest Tennessee (Chester, Decatur, Hardin, Hardeman, Haywood, Henderson, Madison, McNairy counties) needing home-based support.[2]
programs.whats_unique: State-funded home care services available even to non-Medicaid eligible individuals, distinguishing it from purely federal programs by offering broader access via Tennessee's AAAD network.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Comprehensive in-home services including meals and personal care; part of a regional system serving multiple counties.[2]

programs.name: TennCare Choices
programs.what_it_does: TennCare Choices delivers Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) to help seniors and disabled adults with daily activities, medical care, and essential support for aging in place. It allows waiver of nursing home placement in favor of lower-level home care for eligible participants.[3][4]
programs.who_its_for: Tennessee residents aged 20+ needing nursing home-level care but preferring home or assisted living, financially eligible for TennCare (income/asset limits apply, e.g., 2022 guidelines).[3][4]
programs.whats_unique: Tennessee's Medicaid-managed HCBS waiver program under TennCare, providing state-specific home-based alternatives to institutional care not universally structured this way in other states.[3][4]
programs.benefit_value: Support for daily living and medical needs to avoid nursing homes; covers those meeting TennCare financial criteria.[3][4]

programs.name: Single Point of Entry (SPOE)
programs.what_it_does: SPOE acts as a one-stop shop for information and access to long-term care programs and services for the elderly and adults with physical disabilities. It facilitates seamless connections to Tennessee's array of aging services, including those for non-Medicaid qualifiers.[2]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors and adults with physical disabilities in Southwest Tennessee seeking long-term care resources.[2]
programs.whats_unique: Tennessee's designated statewide access point through AAADs for both Medicaid and non-Medicaid services, streamlining entry into state-specific programs beyond federal standards.[2]
programs.benefit_value: Centralized referral to home care, meals, and other services; toll-free access at 1-866-836-6678.[2]

programs.name: Aging Nutrition Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides congregate meals at senior centers and home-delivered meals through programs like Meals on Wheels and Senior Dine-In, reaching nearly two million meals annually. Meals meet Dietary Guidelines for Americans and support active community participation.[6][8]
programs.who_its_for: Persons aged 60 and over in Tennessee, particularly in regions like Upper Cumberland and statewide via HRAs and senior centers.[6][8]
programs.whats_unique: Tennessee's state-administered nutrition initiative via HRAs and AAADs, extending beyond federal OAA funding with local delivery like four weekly congregate meals.[6][8]
programs.benefit_value: Almost 2 million meals per year; nutritious noon meals 4 days/week at centers.[6][8]
